explicit_text: I live in Lexington, Kentucky, and I really appreciate the beautiful environment here, especially living on what used to be a horse farm. I worked as the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington, starting right when the city went under an EPA consent decree in the early 2000s due to unmanaged storm water issues. It was a rewarding job because I could see the direct impact of environmental initiatives at the local level, like improving sewer systems and starting recycling programs.

entity: Kentucky Climate Change Oral History project
explicit_text: This interview is part of the Kentucky Climate Change Oral History project, which I participated in on July 10th, 2023. The project is important for documenting environmental experiences and histories in Kentucky, and I was happy to share my story and work related to environmental protection and policy in the state. It was conducted by Zada Komara, a faculty member at the University of Kentucky.

explicit_text: I worked for Procter & Gamble for almost 24 years right out of college, mostly in manufacturing roles like engineering, operations, and maintenance. Mid-career, I took on environmental permitting and safety programs, which I really enjoyed because it combined my social work and engineering backgrounds. Procter & Gamble was supportive, promoting women and providing extensive training and certifications in environmental areas.

explicit_text: Lloyd Cress was the Kentucky state commissioner for the environment when I joined as a special projects manager and technical advisor. I loved working with him and in the public sector, helping with education, regulations, and programs like Kentucky EXCEL. When Lloyd was promoted, I was honored to be promoted into his role as commissioner, becoming the first female commissioner for Kentucky’s environment.

explicit_text: Kentucky is my home state where I was born and spent much of my life and career. From my early days in coal communities to working as the state environmental commissioner, I have been deeply involved in Kentucky’s environmental issues. Representing Kentucky on a national level and working with EPA Region 4 was a significant part of my career.

explicit_text: As Kentucky’s environmental commissioner, I regularly met with EPA Region 4 leaders, which includes several southern states. These meetings were eye-opening and showed me how much opportunity there is to improve environmental conditions through cooperation. It was an important part of representing Kentucky on the national environmental stage.

explicit_text: Ernie Fletcher was the Republican governor I worked for as Kentucky’s environmental commissioner. Despite our different political views—I'm a liberal Democrat—he supported many environmental initiatives and judged my work on its merits, which I appreciated. His leadership allowed me to focus on environmental progress without partisan interference.

explicit_text: Jim Newberry hired me to be the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington after Governor Fletcher’s term ended. He was a magnificent person to work for, and I valued the opportunity to impact local environmental policies directly. Working under his leadership was one of the finest jobs I ever had.

explicit_text: I served as the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington, starting during a challenging time when the city was under an EPA consent decree due to storm water management failures. My role involved bringing the city into compliance, improving sewer systems, launching recycling programs, and implementing energy and air quality initiatives. It was rewarding to see tangible improvements in Fayette County through my work.

It was rewarding to see tangible improvements in the county through my work.

explicit_text: The City of Lexington went under an EPA consent decree in the early 2000s after two young women drowned due to unmanaged storm water near the football stadium. I took over as environmental commissioner at that time to help the city comply with federal law and improve storm water and sewer systems. The decree pushed us to develop proper maps and infrastructure to prevent such tragedies and environmental hazards.

explicit_text: Fayette County is where Lexington is located, and during my time as environmental commissioner, we implemented the first energy and air programs there. We also bought new garbage trucks and started a recycling program to improve waste management. It was fulfilling to contribute to environmental improvements in Fayette County.
implicit_text: The county where the city I worked in is located was where we implemented the first energy and air programs during my time as environmental commissioner.
